text: Prospective memory failures are not random — they cluster around transitions. You forget to take the pill when you leave the kitchen; you forget to post the letter when you exit the house. These are boundary failures, and they arise because the brain uses spatial and temporal context as a retrieval scaffold. When context shifts, the cues associated with an intention can become temporarily inaccessible. Gabriel Radvansky's event segmentation theory helps explain this: each bounded episode in experience is stored as a coherent 'event model,' and crossing a boundary triggers an update that can suppress information from the prior episode — the cognitive mechanism behind the famous 'doorway effect.'
question: What does Gabriel Radvansky's event segmentation theory suggest causes boundary-related memory failures?

options.text: Spatial anxiety disrupts the hippocampus during transitions.
Working memory is overloaded by new environmental stimuli.
Crossing a boundary triggers an update that can suppress prior episode information.
The prefrontal cortex deprioritizes intentions when locomotion begins.
